item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yea, I like this. Some Japanese-inspired stuff is always nice, especially when connected to Japanese mythology, and I like the idea that they're literal phantoms born of mass suffering, cursed to wander the Earth in anguish, feeding off the blood of the living. Cute.

My only criticism is it's classification. I think Keter is a bit much for these things. While they can't be destroyed or reasoned with, the Foundation seems to have them pretty locked down. They've got a good and effective system for temporarily neutralising them, there haven't been any deaths since Halloween 2008, and it only affects Japan. Worthy of Euclid, but not Keter imo.

Also, very minor point:

Despite many failed attempts, no attempt at communication has been successful

Writing 'attempt' twice that close together doesn't read very well. Maybe change one of them.

Despite that though, I especially like the fridge horror that comes when you think about what it would be like to be a victim of these things. Just imagine, you are walking alone at night, going home, or going for a walk. Then, just for a second, you hear a rattlling. It's faint, just enough to make you look around, but you chalk it up to the wind in the trees and move on. But then maybe you feel a chill, maybe you realise there is no wind, and then you turn around and there's a motherfucking 30 metre tall skeleton standing above you. You run in blind panic and fear but it chases, easily catching up to you, and then grasping you in it's cold, hard, skeleton hands. The last things you see are the monster's fractured bones and broken jaw, opening like a clamp and placing your head inside. You feel a great pressure on your neck, and then darkness.

So yeah, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat. Seeing the phrase Youkai Battalion immediately makes me want to know more about its story, while also vaguely reminding me of one of my favorite anime (Otome Youkai Zakuro).
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read it more as "if we acknowledge this we'll be admitting we did something horrible, which we totally didn't". Imperial Japan was racist af, and I wouldn't put it past them to pretend the suffering of chinese people's literally just "didn't count".

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent edit was reverted, as it was an unauthorized edit. As per the Site Rules:

* Editing Other Articles: You may make minor edits for grammar or spelling to other peoples' articles. Please fill out the 'Short description of changes' box. Major edits require permission from the original author or staff. Stylistic and tonal edits are considered major edits.

Blackboxing content counts as a stylistic edit, and as such should not be done without permission from the original author or staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you could expand on the Youkai battalion concept a little more.

Since I'm the guy who namedropped 'Youkai Battalion' in the first place, you can go to the IJAMEA Hub to learn a little bit more about it.

Seeing as you are interested in the 2nd Sino-Japanese War, the aforementioned hub does have some description of Foundation-verse Imperial Japan's activities during that period.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a history buff whose main interest is in the Second Sino-Japanese War, I really like this idea.

Going through your entry, I'd like to share my interpretation of the SCP:

  • I've heard about this Youkai before, but your take on it is interesting.
  • It clearly represents the suffering of those affected by Japan's actions, and later Japan's own suffering for the next two sightings.
  • The 1938 skeleton clearly first represents the victims of the Nanjing Massacre. It being seen for the next years of the war shows it has absorbed the suffering of other Chinese in mass killings; the Zhejiang-Jiangxi campaign, Unit 731, the Three Alls campaign, etc. Its large size represents the colossal Chinese civilian death toll. Damage to the pelvis represents the way in which women were often killed by the Japanese. Damage to the skull could represent being hit with a rifle stock or decapitations. Damage to the ribs is from bayonets, reflecting how Japanese recruits performed bayonet practice on live prisoners or how most victims of Imperial Japan were killed by bayonets.
  • Eventually sightings pop up all across the Pacific and Southeast Asia, as the suffering of all those under Japanese occupation can't be represented by just one skeleton.
  • The 1946/54 skeleton's injuries represent the firebombing campaigns done by the Allies in the later years of WW2. Fukuoka was heavily firebombed, which is why the skeleton appeared there. The low levels of radiation are definitely a nod to the atomic bombings.
  • The 2011 skeleton appears on March 11th, to immediately foreshadow the incoming nuclear disaster, tsunami and earthquake over the next few days. Subsequent sightings represent the damage inflicted by the disasters. For the injuries, I've read that radiation poisoning makes your teeth fall out, while the skull damage represents those crushed by rubble in the quake.

All in all, a very good concept and certainly one of my favorites. If there was anything left to improve, you could expand on the Youkai battalion concept a little more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the implication of shame from their behavior to the Nanking instance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree completely. The giant skeletons themselves weren't that interesting to me; they feel like a fairly generic monster, and not a very scary one at that. The historical info, especially the examples of instances born from specific tragedies, was what took this from meh to upvote for me.

One small bit of criticism:

The three additional instances of SCP-2863 catalogued since the Foundation takeover of IJAMEA assets, as detailed below.

Unless I'm missing something, that's not a complete sentence. Maybe remove the comma and change "as" to "are"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Attack On Titan anyone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel this would have been, on the whole, more interesting if left more unconnected to the traditional creatures. Also, while the tie-ins to real events are done tastefully, I don't think they bring that much to the table.

There's certainly some good elements in the concept of a dark lurking giant skeleton with no real purpose other than to kill, but I feel that concept is overshadowed by lots of information that feel like filler.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
okay shit, this one got me. Can't really tell why, I'm not one to be spooked by skeletons. Probably the mix of "Titans", making almost no sound and only being a threat in dark areas, far away from civilization. I also really like the individualism of each of these menacing entities.

Oh and bonus points for being born out of suffering, I dig that concept.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good read. I really like write-ups of mythological creatures taken straight, as a rule. And you certainly add a lot of your own color here, and you touch on some pretty serious themes. Really, just a fun read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was honestly expecting Aiden to pull this one.

Also, no. Into the pun wiggler with you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, seeing as these things are very hard to get rid of, and multiples appear every night, I suppose it's safe to say that MTF-O3 is on a permanent Yokai watch?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I won't necessarily call Nanking "Japanese territory". Japanese-occupied, yes. But I don't think the Imperial Japanese planned to annex China. Maybe Manchukuo is on the "to be annexed" list, but the Imperial Japanese did support a puppet Chinese Republic.

But yeah, I will treat the SCP to be effective on Japanese-occupied lands and that was why the USA/Foundation decided to strip Japan of its empire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a matter of fact, "Gashadokuro" is NOT a typical youkai of Japanese folklore.

In my opinion, this may be a reason why SCP-2863 is unable to be controlled by IJAMEA. It is not the typical youkai species… maybe one retroactively brought into existence and made to always felt like it existed since the past.

I do have vague ideas on what the Youkai Battalion could be, after all…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, pleased to see this here. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I hadn't thought of it that way. I've gotten so used to thinking of Nanking in terms of a Chinese territory, I'd forgotten that the Japanese had actually successfully claimed it during the war. That makes much more sense now. I was beginning to wonder if these things were spawned literally any time the Japanese were involved in large scale warfare. Though, given the history of Japan as a relatively insular island culture, a vast majority of their wars would tend to spawn these things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nanking was, at the time, under Japanese control, therefore technically Japanese territory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because the SCP.is a Gashadokuro, a sprit from Japanese Mythology. It would make sense that it follows the Japanese around, for better or worse.

Kinda the same reason you're unlikely to see a Hydra outside of Greece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea here is excellent. Supernatural horror reflecting historical horror - it's a double dose of creep, with the real-life element lending realism. You utilise the historical references very well: "cracks to the pelvis"… Jesus.

Effectively and efficiently written too. Happily upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True, but then we have one spawned by the Nanking massacre in China, so I'm curious if this effect is limited only to massacres created by direct interaction with the Japanese, and if so, I'd kinda like to see a little more hinting as to why it specifically targets Japan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It does very clearly say "within the borders of Japan".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one works quite well for me, but I feel like there's something… missing in it. Almost like it doesn't have any meat on its bones….

…
…
…

I'll show myself out now.

But bad jokes aside, I feel like there could be more of a story here rather than just "Hey these things rose from assorted massacres because *shrug*". I mean, are they endemic only to Asia? Only to Japan and massacres authored by the Japanese? What about in Russia and Germany? I'm fairly certain St. Petersburg, Volgograd, Prokhorovka, Pearl Harbor, Corregidor, and Berlin should have some giant skeletons stalking around if we're following this metric everywhere. If not, maybe emphasize the hints at there being something unique about the area that has these things spawning.

I feel like you have the beginnings to something seriously chilling and original here, but it's just played too straight on the nose for me right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Gashadokuro are a race of malevolent yōkai

This was precisely when I upvoted. Always have to love some Japanese monsters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well. Sort of. While stories about similar giant skeletal creatures stretch back well over a thousand years, technically the first literary reference to Gashadokuro as such was by Saitou in '68, itself based on a Utagawa's 1844 illustration, itself depicting events from the 900s.

However, at this point the Gashadokuro have become so ingrained in both Western and Japanese culture as ancient folkloric spirits that I don't really feel like it makes a difference. It is clearly a work of fiction, as is everything here, so I see no issue with taking a few artistic liberties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It’s really hard and sorry for us to say this, but kidonoi and me found a problem.

As a matter of fact, "Gashadokuro" is NOT a typical youkai of Japanese folklore. It was newly created in 1960s by Morihito Saitou as a creepy fictional monster, based on Kuniyoshi Utagawa's irrelevant illustration. In other words, Gashadokuro was intentionally characterized as "Giant man-eating skeleton that is created by mass death".

We really don't know whether turning a fictional character that have a clear author into a SCP object is appropriate or not… at least, current 2863 is almost the same with Saitou's Gashadokuro.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate that, and I agree. The goofy line has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While a bunch of giant skeletons certainly invite the goofy Andrew Gold references, the Nanking Massacre and the atomic bombing of two Japanese cities do not. There's a lot to love here, but you're going to have to pick one of these tones before I feel comfortable upvoting this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A spooky scary skeleton for Halloween.

Appropriate.

item.title: Retrospective
item.content:encoded:
Last Halloween I posted my first article to the site after years of being a mostly silent lurker. In the year since, I've posted 11 more, met and befriended a ton of great people, become a better author, a better critic, and have had the pleasure on serving on three separate teams as Junior Staff. Thanks for the part y'all have played in my life for the last year, here's to even more.

Happy (almost) Halloween.

Now about the article.
Thanks for reading

Article obviously heavily inspired by MrWrong's similar articles.

Thank you to the dozens of people who helped me with this one, I'd credit you all but I know I'd miss someone.

The three examples at the end are the result of, in order, the massacre at nanking, the atomic bombings, and the tohoku earthquake.

The IJAMEA was created by user Mazyrian in SCP-2937 and has been largely developed by MrWrong (And coincidentally is now one article away from qualifying for GOI status).
